slides.title: Immutable Data 🛡️
slides.content:
Sometimes certain data inside an object should never, ever be changed after it is created. A perfect example is a database `id` or a user's date of birth.

In TypeScript, we use the `readonly` modifier in our Interfaces to enforce this.

slides.title: Using readonly
slides.content: You place the `readonly` keyword directly before the property name in your interface.

quizData.question: What happens if you try to execute `earth.id = 4;` using the interface above?

options.text: It successfully updates the ID to 4.
TypeScript throws a compile-time error because 'id' is readonly.
It changes the ID but deletes the object name.

quizData.explanation: Because it is marked as `readonly`, TS will completely forbid any code from reassigning that specific property after the object is created.

slides.title: Const vs Readonly
slides.content: You might think `const` solves this, but `const` only stops you from replacing the *entire* object variable.

slides.title: Best Practice 🚀
slides.content: Use `const` for variables and `readonly` for properties inside interfaces to lock down your data from accidental tampering.
